# 

# Problem Statement {#problem-statement}

Elderly people in the Klang Valley face complicated route choices, difficult transfers, lack of accessibility information, complex app interfaces, and confusion caused by multiple route options when using public transport (What); the issue mainly affects seniors aged 60 and above with limited mobility and digital literacy who rely on public transport (Who); it occurs at LRT, MRT, KTM and bus interchanges within Kuala Lumpur and the Klang Valley (Where); authoritative media reports highlight that the lack of integrated accessibility information across the Klang Valley’s public transport network leaves seniors in an 'information void' concerning the availability of barrier-free paths. Such unpredictability not only complicates independent travel but also heightens anxiety for the elderly in complex transit hubs and interchanges(Why). Therefore, this study proposes ElderGo KL – an elderly-friendly public transport route planning system dedicated to the Klang Valley that features a senior-adapted interface, provides one single optimal route based on personal preferences to avoid choice overload, and integrates useful information such as accessibility and ticketing details, to lower barriers and improve safety and independent mobility for elderly users.

# User Persona {#user-persona}

## Basic Information {#basic-information}

• Name: Tan Ah Hock

• Age: 66

• Location: Klang Valley

• Background: Retired, lives alone, regularly uses LRT/MRT/bus

• Ability: Weakened eyesight, slow mobility, low digital literacy

• Device: Basic smartphone user

## User Profile {#user-profile}

He heavily depends on public transport for daily activities. With aging eyesight and reduced mobility, he finds it difficult to use complex applications. He prefers routes with less walking, fewer transfers, and better accessibility. He needs a simple, clear, and stable travel planning tool that allows him to travel independently and safely.

## Pain Points {#pain-points}

• Conventional transport apps show too many route options, making it difficult to choose the most suitable one

• Small fonts and buttons make the interface hard to read and operate, leading to misclicks

• Information on whether stations have accessibility facilities (e.g., elevators, ramps) is unclear, making it difficult to know in advance whether a station is suitable for his needs

• Complex transfer guidance increases the risk of getting lost or taking the wrong route

• Senior discounts and ticketing information are scattered and difficult to find

## Frustrations {#frustrations}

• Too many route options create decision overload and stress

• Complex usage steps increase learning difficulty, leading to potential abandonment of the app

• Lack of pre-trip clarity on station accessibility creates uncertainty 

• Existing applications do not support filtering routes based on mobility needs

• Useful travel information is scattered, making it time-consuming to search

## Expectations {#expectations}

• One-tap access to a recommended route without complicated setup

• Only one optimal route is shown to avoid confusion

• Clean interface with large fonts, no ads, and no unnecessary information

• Clear visibility of whether each station has accessibility facilities (e.g., elevators, ramps) before the trip

• System automatically filters routes that are unsuitable based on mobility needs

• All essential information (accessibility, tickets, stations) is available in one place

## Goals {#goals}

• Quickly obtain a single, safe, and suitable recommended route

• Use a simple, elderly-friendly interface with large fonts and minimal steps

• Assess whether a route is suitable for accessibility needs before traveling

• Access all accessibility, ticketing, and station information in one place

• Achieve independent, safe, and smooth public transport travel

# ![][image2]Target Audience {#target-audience}

## Primary Audience {#primary-audience}

| User Group | Key Characteristics | Core Pain Points |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Living-alone elderly** | Aged 60+, living independently, children live far away or are busy with work | No one to help check routes or use smartphones; fear of getting lost when going out alone with no way to ask for help |
| **Low digital literacy elderly** | Can only use basic phone functions (calls, camera, WhatsApp) | Cannot understand icons, pop-ups, or app navigation; unable to copy/paste or download apps |
| **Visually impaired elderly** | Age-related vision decline (presbyopia, post-cataract surgery), require large text and high contrast | Small text is unreadable; dense information is hard to process; frequently mis-tap buttons |
| **Mobility-impaired elderly** | Uses walking stick or walker, has joint pain, cannot climb stairs or walk long distances | Must confirm in advance whether lifts, ramps, or step-free routes are available; needs to avoid stairs and steep slopes |
| **Language-minority users** | Only understand Malay; unable to read English | Cannot use English-based apps; often rely on guessing, leading to confusion and app abandonment due to fear of mistakes |

## Secondary Audience {#secondary-audience}

The above elderly users’ children or caregivers. They need a trustworthy tool to help plan trips for their parents and remotely confirm their safety through sharing features, rather than direct surveillance or monitoring.

# Epics & User Stories {#epics-&-user-stories}

## **EPIC 01 | Global Age-Friendly Navigation and Visual Adaptation System** {#epic-01-|-global-age-friendly-navigation-and-visual-adaptation-system}

**Description:**  
This epic focuses on the system’s basic usability and age-friendly interface capabilities, providing large-text display, English/Bahasa Melayu switching, and persistent global bottom navigation in a unified way. It addresses common issues faced by elderly users in Klang Valley when using conventional travel apps, such as text being too small, buttons being difficult to tap, interfaces being hard to understand, and getting lost after performing actions. Through a stable, clear, and low-learning-cost global interaction framework, this epic reduces the first layer of barriers users face when entering the system.

**Purpose:**  
The core purpose of this epic is to solve three fundamental pain points first: “cannot see clearly,” “cannot understand clearly,” and “cannot find the way.” It establishes a unified, reliable, and predictable interaction experience for subsequent functions. It is not a single-page feature, but the underlying prerequisite that enables elderly users to use the whole system smoothly.

### **US1.1 – One-Tap Switching Between Fixed Large-Text Modes** {#us1.1-–-one-tap-switching-between-fixed-large-text-modes}

**As** an elderly user with weaker eyesight,  
**I want** to switch to a more readable large-text mode with one tap,  
**so that** I can clearly read buttons, text, and route information.

**AC1.1.1: Switch font size from standard mode to large mode**  
Scenario: The user enlarges text to improve readability  
Given the system is currently in the standard text-size display mode and the page has loaded normally  
When the user taps the font switch button at the top of the page or another globally visible font switch control once  
Then the system should immediately switch the main text, button labels, form field titles, navigation text, and route information across the site to large-text display mode, while ensuring there is no horizontal scrolling, no content clipping, and all key buttons remain clearly readable and tappable

**AC1.1.2: Switch font size from large mode to extra-large mode**  
Scenario: The user needs a larger font size for further readability improvement  
Given the system is currently in large-text display mode  
When the user taps the font switch button again  
Then the system should switch all site text to extra-large display mode, while keeping the page layout stable, with no horizontal scrolling, no content clipping, and all key interactive elements remaining tappable

**AC1.1.3: Cycle font size from extra-large mode back to standard mode**  
Scenario: The user is already at the maximum font size and wants to return to the default reading size  
Given the system is currently in extra-large text display mode  
When the user taps the font switch button again  
Then the system should restore all site text to standard display mode, keep the button appearance consistent, show no extra prompt or modal, and maintain a stable layout without content misalignment

**AC1.1.4: Preserve the selected font setting after refresh**  
Scenario: The user re-enters the system after changing the font size  
Given the user has previously switched the font mode and completed one normal usage session  
When the user refreshes the current page or closes and re-enters the system  
Then the system should automatically restore the last used font mode without requiring the user to set it again, and the restored layout should remain stable and consistent

**AC1.1.5: Maintain readability and operability on content-heavy pages**  
Scenario: The user continues browsing long-content pages in large-text mode  
Given the current page contains a large amount of content or dense information, such as a route result page, help page, or station detail page  
When the user switches to large-text mode and continues browsing the content  
Then the page layout should automatically adapt to the enlarged text, ensuring paragraphs are clear, information blocks do not overlap, primary buttons are not obscured, and the user can continue reading and tapping without zooming the page

### **US1.2 – One-Tap Switching Between English and Bahasa Melayu Interfaces** {#us1.2-–-one-tap-switching-between-english-and-bahasa-melayu-interfaces}

**As** an elderly user who is more comfortable with my native-language interface,  
**I want** to switch between English and Bahasa Melayu with one tap,  
**so that** I can understand the system throughout the whole journey.

**AC1.2.1: Do not lose entered content when switching language**  
Scenario: The user switches the interface language after filling in route information  
Given the user has already entered some content in the origin, destination, or other input fields  
When the user taps the language switch button to change the interface from English to Bahasa Melayu, or from Bahasa Melayu to English  
Then the system should immediately update the navigation, buttons, titles, prompts, and explanatory text on the current page, while preserving the user’s entered content, current page position, and the state of expanded modules, without triggering a full-page reset

**AC1.2.2: Remember the user’s last-used language**  
Scenario: The user enters the system again  
Given the user has previously completed browsing or actions successfully in a certain language version  
When the user enters the system again later  
Then the system should load the homepage and subsequent pages in the previously selected language by default, reducing the burden of repeated language switching

### **US1.3 – Persistent Global Bottom Navigation** {#us1.3-–-persistent-global-bottom-navigation}

**As** an elderly user who easily gets lost within pages,  
**I want** to switch between core functions through bottom navigation on any page,  
**so that** I can always return to the page I need.

**AC1.3.1: The user can access core modules from any page**  
Scenario: The user switches modules through the bottom navigation  
Given the user is currently on any main page or subpage, such as a route result page, station detail page, or help content page  
When the user taps Planning, Stations, Preference or Help in the bottom navigation  
Then the system should immediately navigate to the homepage of the corresponding module, so that the user knows which module they are currently in

## **EPIC 02 | Personalized Travel Preference Configuration System** {#epic-02-|-personalized-travel-preference-configuration-system}

**Description:**  
This epic supports elderly users in setting personalized preferences based on their mobility condition and travel habits, including accessibility first, less walking, and fewer transfers. Considering that many elderly users find complex route-filtering logic difficult to understand and do not want to repeat settings every time they use the system, this epic allows the system to adapt proactively to the user through lightweight guidance and cached memory, rather than requiring the user to repeatedly adapt to the system.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to move the judgment of “what suits me” into the system logic, helping elderly users continuously receive route recommendations that better match their physical condition and preferences without needing to understand complex route comparisons. This reduces anxiety, lowers cognitive burden, and improves travel safety and confidence.

### **US2.1 – First-Time Preference Setup Guidance** {#us2.1-–-first-time-preference-setup-guidance}

**As** an elderly user who is not familiar with complex settings,  
**I want** to be simply asked whether I want to set preferences when I first enter route planning,  
**so that** I can quickly adjust my travel preferences and let the system help optimize my route.

**AC2.1.1: Show a one-time preference setup popup when entering Planning for the first time**  
Scenario: A first-time user enters the route-planning page  
Given the user enters the homepage for the first time and the system has not recorded any preference settings  
When the page remains open for a short time and the basic content has finished loading  
Then the system should display a one-time, lightweight, and easy-to-understand preference guidance prompt with clear ‘Save’ and ‘Cancel’ options, and the prompt text should be simple and not use complex terminology

**AC2.1.2: Display editable preference options directly in the popup**  
Scenario: The user views the first-time preference setup popup  
Given the first-time preference popup is displayed  
When the user reviews the popup content  
Then the popup should directly show editable preference options, including Accessibility First, Least Walking, and Fewest Transfers, allowing the user to modify the options without entering a separate setup flow

**AC2.1.3: Save selected preferences from the popup**  
Scenario: The user saves preference choices from the first-time popup  
Given the user has modified or reviewed the preference options in the popup  
When the user clicks Save  
Then the system should save the selected preference configuration, apply it to later route recommendations, close the popup, and allow the user to continue using route planning without losing any current page state

**AC2.1.4: Cancel preference setup and continue with default mode**  
Scenario: The user cancels the first-time preference setup  
Given the first-time preference popup is displayed  
When the user clicks Cancel or closes the popup  
Then the the system should close the popup, continue in default no-preference mode, keep route planning usable, and avoid showing the same popup again during the current planning visit

**AC2.1.5: Preference popup failure should not affect normal usage**  
Scenario: The preference setup popup cannot load properly  
Given the preference setup popup fails to load due to network or front-end issues  
When the user enters the route-planning page for the first time  
Then the system should directly allow the user to continue using route planning in default mode, without showing a blank overlay, a non-clickable state, or forced waiting

### US2.2 – Preference Saving and Continued Effectiveness {#us2.2-–-preference-saving-and-continued-effectiveness}

**As** an elderly user with fixed travel needs,  
**I want** the system to remember my preference settings,  
**so that** they are automatically applied in later route planning.

**AC2.2.1: Automatically apply saved preferences in later planning**  
Scenario: The user plans a route again after saving preferences  
Given the user has completed preference selection and saved successfully  
When the user enters route planning again later and starts a search  
Then the system should automatically apply those preferences in the route recommendation logic, without requiring the user to revisit the settings page and select them again

**AC2.2.2: Preserve preference state continuously within the cache validity period**  
Scenario: The user repeatedly uses the system over a period of time  
Given the user’s preference settings have not been actively modified and the cache is still valid  
When the user enters the system multiple times later or repeatedly plans routes  
Then the system should continue to retain and use the same preference settings, ensuring recommendation results remain consistent and predictable

**AC2.2.3: Return to default mode and re-guide after cache expiry**  
Scenario: The user enters the system again after the cache has expired  
Given the preference cache has expired, been cleared, or cannot be read  
When the user re-enters the system and visits the Planning page  
Then the system should revert to default no-preference mode and re-trigger the first-time preference guidance at an appropriate time so that the user can choose again

### **US2.3 – Preference Modification Entry** {#us2.3-–-preference-modification-entry}

**As** an elderly user whose needs may change,  
**I want** to still be able to modify my preferences later,  
**so that** the system’s recommendations continue to match my actual needs.

**AC2.3.1: The user can modify preference items from the preference page**  
Scenario: The user enters the preference page to make adjustments  
Given the user enters the preference configuration page from bottom navigation  
When the user opens the preference page  
Then the system should allow the user to view, enable, disable, or recombine existing preference items, and clearly present the currently active configuration state

**AC2.3.2: New preferences take effect immediately in later recommendations after saving**  
Scenario: The user completes preference adjustment and saves  
Given the user has modified the original preferences and clicked Save  
When the user performs route planning again afterward  
Then the system should use the new preferences for route filtering and scoring instead of continuing to use the old configuration

**AC2.3.3: Setting page failure should not affect continued use of existing preferences**  
Scenario: The user tries to modify preferences but the page fails to load  
Given the preference settings page fails to load successfully or the save request fails  
When the user attempts to modify preferences  
Then the system should prompt the user to try again later and continue retaining the still-valid existing preferences, avoiding abnormal recommendation logic caused by setting failure

## **EPIC 03 | Route Planning Input and Time Selection System** {#epic-03-|-route-planning-input-and-time-selection-system}

**Description:**  
This epic covers the key input flow before route generation, including origin and destination entry, current location usage, Google autocomplete, and travel time selection. It mainly addresses issues such as elderly users making spelling errors when entering places, not understanding the operation steps clearly, and not knowing what to do next after searching.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to make the route-planning entry sufficiently clear, simple, and low-pressure so that elderly users can complete necessary information entry with minimal effort, while also allowing the system to obtain enough contextual conditions before actually generating a route, thereby improving recommendation accuracy and comprehensibility.

### US3.1 – Simplified Origin and Destination Input {#us3.1-–-simplified-origin-and-destination-input}

**As** an elderly user who is not good at entering complex place names,  
**I want** the system to help me quickly complete the origin and destination input,  
**so that** I can start route planning smoothly.

**AC3.1.1: Provide autocomplete suggestions when entering a location**  
Scenario: The user enters a place on the homepage  
Given the user is currently on the homepage and the origin or destination input field is editable  
When the user starts entering a place keyword  
Then the system should call Google autocomplete and display a list of candidate places, and the candidates should be clear, tappable, easy to read, and reduce the burden of fully spelling out the place name

**AC3.1.2: Proceed to the next step when the origin and destination are valid**  
Scenario: The user has completed origin and destination entry  
Given the origin and destination are both valid places recognized by the system  
When the user clicks Search  
Then the system should pass input validation and proceed to the time-selection step, rather than directly reporting an error or remaining on the same page without feedback

**AC3.1.3: Prevent continuation and provide clear guidance when input is incomplete**  
Scenario: The user attempts to search when fields are incomplete  
Given the origin or destination is empty, invalid, or has not been confirmed from a recognizable candidate  
When the user clicks Search  
Then the system should prompt the user to complete or correct the input, clearly indicate which field has the issue, and block progression to the next step to avoid generating an incorrect route

### **US3.2 – Select Travel Time After Search** {#us3.2-–-select-travel-time-after-search}

**As** an elderly user who needs to consider travel timing in advance,  
**I want** to explicitly choose a travel time after searching,  
**so that** the system can provide a more suitable recommendation result.

**AC3.2.1: Show the time selection page after search**  
Scenario: The user enters time selection after completing place input  
Given the user has entered a valid origin and destination and clicked Search  
When the system completes basic input validation  
Then it should open the Plan Your Time page instead of directly entering the route result page, ensuring the user explicitly understands the current travel time context

**AC3.2.2: Provide complete and easy-to-understand time options**  
Scenario: The user views available travel time labels  
Given the time-selection page has been displayed successfully  
When the user views the available options  
Then the system should provide four clear options—Now, Morning, Afternoon, and Evening—and use understandable wording or visual separation to help elderly users understand their meanings

**AC3.2.3: Start the recommendation flow after time confirmation**  
Scenario: The user completes time selection  
Given the user has selected one time label  
When the user clicks Confirm or Continue  
Then the system should start executing the route recommendation logic based on the origin, destination, selected time, and any existing preference conditions, while also providing appropriate loading feedback

## **EPIC 04 | Single-Route Recommendation and Rule-Based Scoring System** {#epic-04-|-single-route-recommendation-and-rule-based-scoring-system}

**Description:**  
This epic is responsible for transforming user input into one final recommended route. The system combines candidate routes returned by Google, user preferences, and time labels for rule-based scoring, and returns only one optimal result in order to avoid the cognitive burden and choice anxiety that multiple-route comparison creates for elderly users.

**Purpose:**  
The core purpose of this epic is to move the task of “choosing which route” from the user into the system, so that elderly users do not need to face a large number of options, complex parameters, or multi-dimensional trade-offs. Through single-route output, the system helps users make decisions faster and travel with more confidence.

### **US4.1 – Output Only One Optimal Route** {#us4.1-–-output-only-one-optimal-route}

**As** an elderly user who is easily distracted by multiple route options,  
**I want** the system to provide only one route that suits me best,  
**so that** I do not need to compare and judge by myself.

**AC4.1.1: Display only one recommended route after calculation is completed**  
Scenario: The user completes the main route-planning flow  
Given the user has completed origin, destination, and time selection, and the system already has the conditions required to generate a route  
When route calculation is completed  
Then the page should display only one recommended route result, and the user interface should not contain any other alternative route cards, multi-option switching entries, or comparison panels

**AC4.1.2: Hide internal candidate routes and do not require the user to filter them manually**  
Scenario: The third-party service returns multiple candidate routes  
Given the internal calculation logic returns multiple candidate routes  
When the system completes internal rule-based scoring and filtering  
Then the user interface should still present only one recommended route, without requiring the user to compare, rank, understand, or confirm again

**AC4.1.3: Provide an alternative route and corresponding prompt when user needs cannot be fully met**  
Scenario: No suitable route can be generated under the current conditions  
Given there is no available route that meets the conditions, or the route-generation process fails  
When the system cannot return a route result that satisfies the user’s preference settings  
Then the page should clearly indicate that the current route cannot meet the requirements and provide the closest alternative route to the user’s needs

### **US4.2 – Preferences and Time Jointly Participate in Route Filtering** {#us4.2-–-preferences-and-time-jointly-participate-in-route-filtering}

**As** an elderly user with limited mobility or clear travel needs,  
**I want** the system to automatically filter routes based on my preferences and travel time,  
**so that** I get a more suitable plan every time.

**AC4.2.1: Include enabled user preferences in route score calculation**  
Scenario: A user with configured preferences requests a route  
Given the user has enabled one or more preference options  
When the system performs route filtering and scoring  
Then the system should incorporate factors such as accessibility first, least walking, and fewest transfers into its internal weighting logic, and ensure the final result reflects the overall direction of those preferences

**AC4.2.2: Use the default recommendation strategy when preferences are not enabled**  
Scenario: The user plans a route in no-preference mode  
Given the user has not enabled any preference settings  
When the system generates a route  
Then the system should return a recommendation using the default general strategy and should not block route generation due to missing preferences

**AC4.2.3: Automatically balance conflicting preferences internally**  
Scenario: The user selects preferences that may conflict with each other  
Given the user’s preference factors may conflict, for example when least walking and fewest transfers cannot both be optimally satisfied at the same time  
When the system performs scoring  
Then the system should automatically make trade-offs and balance the factors internally, still outputting only one result and not passing the conflict decision to the user

**AC4.2.4: Automatically fall back to default mode when preference data is abnormal**  
Scenario: Preference data is incomplete or invalid  
Given the stored preference data is missing, malformed, or unreadable  
When the system calls the route recommendation logic  
Then the system should automatically fall back to default mode and continue generating a route, avoiding total recommendation failure caused by abnormal preference data

## **EPIC 05 | Route Result Visualization, Offline Saving, and Safe Sharing System** {#epic-05-|-route-result-visualization,-offline-saving,-and-safe-sharing-system}

**Description:**  
This epic focuses on how the recommended route is presented and used after generation, including text view, map view, route summary, accessibility information, weather and rush-hour reminders, as well as offline saving and family sharing capabilities. It responds to the real pain points elderly users face, such as not understanding maps, not remembering routes, worrying about losing network connection during the trip, and wanting family members to know their travel arrangements.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to help elderly users truly understand, retain, and safely use a route after they have obtained it. It not only improves result readability, but also strengthens pre-trip certainty, in-trip reliability, and family-level reassurance and companionship.

### **US5.1 – Switch Between Text View and Map View** {#us5.1-–-switch-between-text-view-and-map-view}

**As** an elderly user who may not necessarily understand maps well,  
**I want** to switch between text mode and map mode for route display,  
**so that** I can view the route in the way that is easiest for me to understand.

**AC5.1.1: Show Text View by default on the result page and provide a Map View entry**  
Scenario: The user opens a generated route result  
Given the user has successfully generated a route and entered the result page  
When the page finishes loading for the first time  
Then the system should display Text View by default, allowing the user to first see textual route information that is easier to understand, while also providing a clear and visible Map View switch entry

**AC5.1.2: Switch views without full-page refresh and keep the same route content**  
Scenario: The user switches between text view and map view  
Given the user is currently on the route result page and clicks the view-switch control  
When the system switches between Text View and Map View  
Then the switch should be completed without a full-page refresh, and both views should display the same recommended route, without route inconsistency or loss of user context

**AC5.1.3: Preserve a usable text-view fallback when map loading fails**  
Scenario: The map service is temporarily unavailable  
Given the map fails to load, times out, or third-party map resources cannot be returned  
When the user switches to Map View  
Then the system should display a clear error message and allow the user to quickly return to Text View, ensuring the user can still continue viewing the complete route through text mode

### **US5.2 – View Route Summary and Step-by-Step Information** {#us5.2-–-view-route-summary-and-step-by-step-information}

**As** an elderly user who needs to quickly understand key route information,  
**I want** to see the summary first and then view details step by step,  
**so that** I can clearly know whether the whole route suits me.

**AC5.2.1: Display route summary information at the top of the result page**  
Scenario: The user first quickly reviews the key points of the route  
Given the route has been generated successfully and the result page is open  
When the user views the top section of the page  
Then the system should display key information such as origin to destination, total travel time, number of transfers, and total walking distance, so the user can judge overall route suitability without reading every step first

**AC5.2.2: Display route details step by step in Text View**  
Scenario: The user reads the route through text  
Given the user is currently in Text View  
When the user starts reviewing the route steps  
Then the system should clearly display each step in sequence, including transport mode, start and end segment, estimated duration, and distance, and should use concise, age-friendly wording to help the user understand what to do next

### **US5.3 – Transparent Display of Accessibility and Risk Information** {#us5.3-–-transparent-display-of-accessibility-and-risk-information}

**As** an elderly user who worries that the route may not suit my needs,  
**I want** to know before departure whether this route and related stations support accessibility, and also see weather and rush-hour reminders,  
**so that** I can set off with greater confidence.

**AC5.3.1: Show whether the route passes through stations or facilities that support accessibility**  
Scenario: The user confirms route suitability before departure  
Given the route result has already been generated successfully  
When the system reads route and station-related data  
Then the page should clearly indicate whether the route passes through stations or facilities that support accessibility, using clear labels, icons, or explanatory text to help the user quickly recognize whether the route is more suitable

**AC5.3.2: Mark accessibility status based on accessibility fields in the route data**  
Scenario: The system shows station support status according to returned accessibility information  
Given Google Route API returns transit-related accessibility fields  
When the page displays route details and station information  
Then the system should use those fields to mark the accessibility support status of the corresponding stations and should not infer data that was not returned

**AC5.3.3: Show weather and rush-hour reminders on the result page without changing the result**  
Scenario: The user learns travel risks while viewing the route  
Given there are weather changes, rush-hour periods, or other states requiring reminders  
When the result page finishes loading  
Then the system should display weather and Rush Hour reminders in a prominent but not overly disruptive position to help the user form expectations, but should not automatically replace the generated route result because of those reminders

**AC5.3.4: Explicitly indicate limited information when accessibility data is incomplete**  
Scenario: The system cannot fully confirm accessibility  
Given the accessibility data of some stations or facilities is missing, unverified, or insufficient  
When the page displays the result  
Then the system should clearly label the information as “limited information” or “not yet verified,” so the user understands the uncertainty instead of assuming accessibility support or non-support by default

### **US5.4 – Save Route Offline** {#us5.4-–-save-route-offline}

**As** an elderly user who may not always have stable network access,  
**I want** to save the route to my phone,  
**so that** I can still view it without a network connection.

**AC5.4.1: Save the current route as a downloadable image**  
Scenario: The user saves the route for offline viewing  
Given the user has generated a route and is currently in Text View or Map View  
When the user clicks Save  
Then the system should generate a savable image version of the current route and provide a clear save or download entry, ensuring the user can still view core route content while offline

**AC5.4.2: Successful saving should not affect current result-page usage**  
Scenario: The user continues browsing after saving the route  
Given the user has successfully completed route saving  
When the user returns to the result page or stays on the current page  
Then the system should preserve the current route content, view state, and browsing position, avoiding page reset or return to the homepage caused by the save action

**AC5.4.3: Show a permission-related prompt when saving fails and keep the page stable**  
Scenario: The device cannot complete the save operation  
Given device storage permission is insufficient, browser download is restricted, or the save process fails abnormally  
When the user attempts to save the route  
Then the system should clearly show an understandable message such as “Unable to save, please check storage permission,” while ensuring the current page does not crash, go blank, or lose the generated route

### **US5.5 – Family Safety Sharing** {#us5.5-–-family-safety-sharing}

**As** an elderly user or a family member,  
**I want** to share the route with family,  
**so that** my family knows my travel arrangement and can confirm my safety.

**AC5.5.1: Generate a shareable route link**  
Scenario: The user wants to send the current route to family members  
Given the user has generated a usable route  
When the user clicks Share  
Then the system should generate a shareable link and provide at least WhatsApp one-tap sharing and copy-link options, making it convenient for users with different sharing habits

**AC5.5.2: Start the sharing flow through WhatsApp**  
Scenario: The user chooses WhatsApp as the sharing channel  
Given the user selects WhatsApp in the sharing options  
When the system launches the sharing flow  
Then the system should correctly pass the current route link into the corresponding sharing entry, reducing the complexity of manual copying and pasting

**AC5.5.3: Sharing failure should not affect continued local route viewing**  
Scenario: Unstable network prevents share-link generation  
Given the network is unavailable or share-link generation fails  
When the user attempts to perform a sharing action  
Then the system should display a message such as “Unable to generate a share link” or an equivalent explanation, while still allowing the user to continue viewing and saving the current route locally without affecting other functions

## **EPIC 06 | Station Information Search and Discovery System** {#epic-06-|-station-information-search-and-discovery-system}

**Description:**  
This epic provides an independent station information module, including popular station recommendations, keyword search, and station detail pages. It mainly responds to elderly users’ uncertainty before travel about questions such as “Does this station have accessibility facilities?”, “Is there a ticket counter?”, and “What are the operating hours?”, as well as their unfamiliarity with complex map-based interaction.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to allow elderly users to confirm in a simple and direct way whether a target station is suitable for their use before they actually set off, thereby reducing hesitation, anxiety, and wrong travel decisions caused by opaque information.

### **US6.1 – Popular Station Recommendations and Search** {#us6.1-–-popular-station-recommendations-and-search}

**As** an elderly user who is not good at typing or cannot remember the exact station name,  
**I want** the system to provide popular stations and search functionality,  
**so that** I can quickly find the target station.

**AC6.1.1: Display popular stations on the Stations page**  
Scenario: The user enters the station discovery module  
Given the user enters the Stations page  
When the page finishes loading  
Then the system should display the Top 4 popular stations, and ensure the cards or list are clear, readable, and easy to tap, so that users who are not familiar with typing can still quickly begin their query

**AC6.1.2: Enter the detail page after clicking a popular station**  
Scenario: The user enters a specific station from the recommendation list  
Given the user clicks any popular station  
When the click action is triggered  
Then the system should enter that station’s detail page and display its related basic services and accessibility information, rather than remaining without feedback

**AC6.1.3: Return usable station search results based on keywords**  
Scenario: The user looks up a target station by keyword  
Given the user enters a station name or keyword in the search box  
When the search is triggered  
Then the system should return matching results and allow the user to further click into the detail page, reducing the pressure of manually locating stations on a map

**AC6.1.4: Show a prompt when there is no exact match**  
Scenario: The user’s input is incomplete or misspelled  
Given the search result does not exactly match, or the user’s input spelling is incomplete  
When the system processes the request  
Then the page should clearly indicate that no exact match was found

### **US6.2 – View Station Detail Information** {#us6.2-–-view-station-detail-information}

**As** an elderly user who needs to confirm accessibility and service conditions in advance,  
**I want** to view station details,  
**so that** I can judge whether the station is suitable for me.

**AC6.2.1: Display key station details**  
Scenario: The user enters a station detail page to view information  
Given the user has entered a station detail page  
When the page loads successfully  
Then the system should display key fields such as station name, accessibility support status, ticket counter information, and operating hours, allowing the user to quickly judge whether the station meets their travel needs

**AC6.2.2: Show explicit placeholders for missing fields**  
Scenario: Some station information has not yet been verified or entered  
Given some fields of the current station have no available data  
When the page renders the detail content  
Then the system should show descriptions such as “Not available” or “Not yet verified” for those fields, while keeping the corresponding information module structure, so the user does not mistakenly think the item was omitted or the system is broken

**AC6.2.3: Provide a friendly error state and a way back when the detail request fails**  
Scenario: The station detail page does not load successfully  
Given the station detail request fails, times out, or encounters an interface exception  
When the page cannot fetch data  
Then the system should display a friendly error prompt and provide a clear back button or retry entry, ensuring the user can continue other actions instead of being trapped on an error page

## **EPIC 07 | Help and Senior Fare Benefit Information System** {#epic-07-|-help-and-senior-fare-benefit-information-system}

**Description:**  
This epic centrally carries static content such as ticket guidance, senior discount card explanation, and privacy information, solving elderly users’ problems of ticketing and benefit information being scattered, hard to find, hard to understand, and easy to make mistakes with. Content is organized in a stable, low-risk, and easy-to-read way, reducing dependence on external websites or complex explanatory documents.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to provide elderly users with a one-stop help center that they can revisit repeatedly with low cognitive burden, allowing them to clearly understand how to buy tickets, how to apply for discounts, and how the system handles data before traveling, thereby improving independent travel capability and trust in the system.

### US7.1 – View Ticket Guide {#us7.1-–-view-ticket-guide}

**As** an elderly user who is unfamiliar with the public transport ticket-purchasing process,  
**I want** to view clear ticket guidance,  
**so that** I can independently prepare for travel.

**AC7.1.1: Enter the ticket guide from the Help page**  
Scenario: The user needs to understand ticket-purchasing steps  
Given the user enters the Help page  
When the user clicks Ticket Guide  
Then the system should open a clear and stable illustrated ticket guide page, and ensure the main information can be quickly understood in reading order

**AC7.1.2: Present long content in a simple vertical-reading format**  
Scenario: The user browses lengthy ticket guide content  
Given the ticket guide page contains long content  
When the user scrolls vertically through it  
Then the page should maintain a simple vertical reading format, without complex interactions, horizontal-swiping dependence, or layouts that are prone to accidental touches

**AC7.1.3: Provide an understandable retry prompt when content fails to load**  
Scenario: Help content is temporarily unavailable  
Given the ticket guide content fails to load  
When the user opens the Ticket Guide page  
Then the system should display a friendly failure prompt and a visible retry entry, instead of showing a blank content area or unexplained error information

### US7.2 – View Discount Card Guide {#us7.2-–-view-discount-card-guide}

**As** a user who hopes to enjoy senior fare discounts,  
**I want** to view discount card application and usage instructions,  
**so that** I clearly understand the benefits and application process.

**AC7.2.1: Enter the discount card explanation page from the Help page**  
Scenario: The user needs to understand senior discounts and card application information  
Given the user enters the Help page  
When the user clicks Discount Card Guide  
Then the system should display illustrated content related to senior benefits, application procedures, and usage methods, helping the user form a complete understanding

**AC7.2.2: Present process-style content in a clear sequence**  
Scenario: The user reads the card application steps  
Given the page content is organized according to the application process  
When the user browses the page  
Then the system should present the explanation in a clear, ordered step-by-step manner, avoiding skipped steps, mixed information, or leaving the user unsure which step they are reading

**AC7.2.3: Support enlarged viewing of illustrative images**  
Scenario: The user needs to see application diagrams or card examples clearly  
Given the page contains explanatory images, diagrams, or sample pictures  
When the user clicks an image  
Then the system should allow the user to enter an enlarged viewing state and provide a clearly visible close or back entry, making it easy for visually weaker users to inspect details and exit quickly

### US7.3 – View Privacy Information {#us7.3-–-view-privacy-information}

**As** a user who cares about how personal data is used,  
**I want** to view the privacy explanation,  
**so that** I understand how the system handles my information.

**AC7.3.1: View readable privacy information from the Help page**  
Scenario: The user actively wants to understand how the system handles data  
Given the user enters the Help page  
When the user opens Privacy Info  
Then the system should display privacy information in a clearly structured and readable way, helping the user understand the system’s basic principles for collecting, using, and storing information

**AC7.3.2: Keep privacy content consistent with the current interface language**  
Scenario: The user checks privacy information again after switching language  
Given the user has already changed the system interface language  
When the user opens Privacy Info again  
Then the privacy content should remain consistent with the current language version, avoiding comprehension difficulties caused by mixing English and Bahasa Melayu

**AC7.3.3: Avoid a blank page when privacy content fails to load**  
Scenario: Privacy information is temporarily unavailable  
Given the page content fails to load because of network or system issues  
When the user attempts to open Privacy Info  
Then the system should show a friendly retry-later prompt and avoid displaying a completely blank page, garbled text, or an unexplained error page

## EPIC 08 | Controlled Conversational AI Travel Assistant System {#epic-08-|-controlled-conversational-ai-travel-assistant-system}

**Description:**  
This epic provides a manually triggered AI Q\&A assistant to help elderly users obtain route guidance, weather advice, station accessibility information, ticket explanations, and privacy information through natural language within the system-supported scope. Unlike open-ended chat, this assistant is strictly limited to the project scope to avoid misleading and unreliable responses.

**Purpose:**  
The purpose of this epic is to provide a more intuitive “ask directly” entry point for elderly users who are not good at actively searching for information across pages, while maintaining information reliability through controlled Q\&A boundaries and reducing confusion and risk caused by incorrect answers, over-speculation, or irrelevant content.

### **US8.1 – Manually Open the AI Assistant** {#us8.1-–-manually-open-the-ai-assistant}

**As** an elderly user who does not want to search everywhere in complex pages for information,  
**I want** to open the AI assistant through a clear button,  
**so that** I can directly ask questions and get help.

**AC8.1.1: Open the AI assistant from supported pages**  
Scenario: The user invokes the AI assistant while browsing the system  
Given the user is currently on the Planning, Stations, Preference or Help page  
When the user clicks the AI icon  
Then the system should open a chat window, ensure the popup position is clear, does not block all primary content, and makes it obvious that the user has entered Q\&A mode

**AC8.1.2: Preserve the current page state when closing the assistant**  
Scenario: The user returns to the original page after finishing the consultation  
Given the user has completed the conversation or is ready to end the consultation  
When the user clicks the close button  
Then the chat window should close, while the current page’s scroll position, input state, and loaded content remain unchanged, without requiring the user to restart previous actions

**AC8.1.3: Keep the main page usable under slow network conditions**  
Scenario: The user attempts to open the assistant under slow network conditions  
Given the current network is slow or the AI entry responds with delay  
When the user attempts to open the assistant  
Then the system should display lightweight loading or a failure prompt, while keeping the main page usable and avoiding a full-page lock or a state that the user cannot return from

### US8.2 – Ask Travel Questions Within the Supported Scope {#us8.2-–-ask-travel-questions-within-the-supported-scope}

**As** an elderly user who is unfamiliar with feature entry points,  
**I want** to ask route, weather, station, and ticket questions in natural language,  
**so that** I can quickly get reliable answers from within the system.

**AC8.2.1: Return structured answers for questions within the supported system scope**  
Scenario: The user asks a travel question already covered by the system  
Given the user asks about routes, weather, station accessibility, ticket information, discount card information, or privacy information  
When the AI receives the input  
Then the system should return a structured and understandable answer based on currently available system data, avoiding overly technical wording

**AC8.2.2: Clarify before answering when the station name is ambiguous**  
Scenario: The user’s question contains an incomplete or ambiguous station name  
Given the station name entered by the user is incomplete, sounds similar to another, or is ambiguous  
When the AI cannot directly determine the exact target  
Then the system should first help the user confirm the subject through a clarification question or similar candidate suggestions, rather than directly returning a possibly incorrect answer

**AC8.2.3: Clearly state missing data without fabricating information**  
Scenario: The information needed for the answer is currently unavailable  
Given the data needed for the question has not yet been provided, has not yet been verified, or cannot be retrieved  
When the AI prepares the reply  
Then the system should clearly state that the information cannot currently be confirmed or is not yet available, and must not fabricate details or make unverified assumptions

### US8.3 – Controlled Scope and Error-Tolerant Guidance {#us8.3-–-controlled-scope-and-error-tolerant-guidance}

**As** an elderly user whose expression may not be very precise,  
**I want** the system to guide me back into the supported scope even if my input is vague,  
**so that** I will not give up using it because my first input failed.

**AC8.3.1: Clarify vague questions or provide button-based guidance**  
Scenario: The user’s input is unclear  
Given the user’s input is vague, incomplete, or lacks key context  
When the AI cannot immediately identify the intent  
Then the system should first try to clarify the question, or provide button-based guidance options such as Routes, Stations, and Help, reducing the burden of rephrasing

**AC8.3.2: Clearly reject out-of-scope questions and guide the user back to supported functions**  
Scenario: The user asks a question unrelated to the system’s goals  
Given the user asks a question outside the system’s supported scope  
When the AI receives the input  
Then the system should clearly explain that it only supports travel-related queries and guide the user back to supported functions such as route planning, station lookup, or help, rather than giving unreliable open-ended answers

**AC8.3.3: Switch to a simpler guidance mode after repeated failures**  
Scenario: Multiple rounds of dialogue still fail to identify the user’s intent  
Given the user repeatedly enters unrecognizable, off-topic, or invalid content  
When the dialogue continues to fail to form effective interaction  
Then the system should switch to a simpler button-based guidance mode to help the user restart from preset entries, rather than continuing with ineffective free-text conversation

## **EPIC 09 | First-Time Onboarding and Low-Barrier Getting Started** {#epic-09-|-first-time-onboarding-and-low-barrier-getting-started}

**Description:**  
This epic is designed for users encountering ElderGo KL for the first time. It provides explanations of the system’s purpose, key functions, example inputs, and a one-tap route-planning entry. It mainly addresses the initial resistance elderly users often face when confronting a new system, such as not knowing how to use it, not daring to tap, being afraid of tapping the wrong thing, and not understanding where to start.

**Purpose:**  
The core purpose of this epic is to help elderly users quickly establish a sense of safety and confidence the first time they open the system, so they understand what the system does, where they should tap first, and that skipping the onboarding will not cause errors, thereby allowing them to enter the truly valuable route-planning flow as soon as possible.

### US9.1 – First-Time Onboarding Page {#us9.1-–-first-time-onboarding-page}

**As** an elderly user using the system for the first time,  
**I want** to first see a concise onboarding explanation when I enter the system,  
**so that** I know what this system can help me do.

**AC9.1.1: Show onboarding only on the first entry**  
Scenario: A first-time user opens the system  
Given the user enters the system for the first time and has not completed onboarding before  
When the homepage finishes loading  
Then the system should display a one-time onboarding page or overlay explaining the system’s core functions and usage, while ensuring the wording is concise and the action path is clear

**AC9.1.2: Do not automatically show onboarding again after completion**  
Scenario: A user who has completed onboarding uses the system again  
Given the user has already completed the first-time onboarding flow  
When the user enters the system again later  
Then the system should not automatically show the same onboarding content again, avoiding repeated disturbance to returning users

**AC9.1.3: Allow onboarding to be skipped without blocking later usage**  
Scenario: The user does not want to read the full onboarding  
Given the user chooses to skip the onboarding on first entry  
When the user continues to the homepage  
Then the system should allow direct use of core functions without restricting Planning, Stations, Help, or other key entries because of the skip action

### US9.2 – Explain Key Functions and Example Inputs {#us9.2-–-explain-key-functions-and-example-inputs}

**As** an elderly user who is not very confident with digital products,  
**I want** to see simple examples and key-button explanations,  
**so that** I know where to start and which button to press.

**AC9.2.1: Explain key functions in onboarding or help overlays**  
Scenario: The user needs to understand the system’s core action entries  
Given the user is currently on the homepage  
When the first-time onboarding or help overlay appears  
Then the system should clearly explain the purpose of key entries such as origin, destination, Search, Stations, and Help, allowing the user to quickly build basic operational understanding

**AC9.2.2: Provide example input or placeholder guidance for place input fields**  
Scenario: The user does not know how to enter a place  
Given the user is unsure how location input should be filled  
When the user views the origin or destination input field  
Then the system should provide example input, placeholder text, or simple guidance, helping the user understand that they can enter a station name, place name, or select from candidate lists

**AC9.2.3: Keep key entries clearly visible after onboarding ends**  
Scenario: The user begins formal use after reading the explanation  
Given the user has completed first-time onboarding or closed the help overlay  
When the user starts operating the system independently  
Then the main entries and core buttons on the page should remain clear, prominent, and recognizable, so the user can continue operating without needing to recall the onboarding content again

### US9.3 – One-Tap Start for Route Planning {#us9.3-–-one-tap-start-for-route-planning}

**As** an elderly user who wants to get started quickly,  
**I want** to enter route planning from the homepage with one tap,  
**so that** I do not have to look for the function in complex menus.

**AC9.3.1: Enter the homepage directly from the homepage main entry**  
Scenario: The user quickly starts route planning from the homepage  
Given the user is currently on the homepage  
When the user clicks the main route-planning entry  
Then the system should directly enter the homepage, without requiring extra menu layers or multiple confirmations, ensuring the starting path is short and clear enough

**AC9.3.2: Preserve later preference onboarding when a first-time user enters Planning**  
Scenario: A first-time user enters the planning flow through the shortcut entry  
Given the user is a first-time user and enters Planning through the homepage main entry  
When the homepage finishes loading  
Then the system should still preserve the later preference-guidance logic, ensuring quick entry and personalized onboarding can coexist
